Facts.text: Byzantine Emperor Basiliscus issues a circular letter (Enkyklikon) to the bishops of his empire, supporting the Monophysite christological position
Catholic Pope Constantine dies after seven years in office
Battle of Liegnitz - Mongolian armies inflict one of the largest defeats in Polish history on Polish and Germans force led by Henry of Silesia with 20-25,000 killed or massacred, including Henry
Battle of Näfels: Swiss canton of Glarus and its allied Old Swiss Confederation defeat the Habsburg forces of the Duchy of Austria
Christopher of Bavaria is appointed King of Denmark (1440-48)
Milan and Venice sign peace of Lodi
Breisach land guardian Peter von Hagenbach throws out Walloon and Italians
Marcello Cervini is elected Pope Marcellus II

Spanish Mendaña and Queirós Expedition departs Callao, Peru, led by Alvaro de Mendan accompanied by 378 men, women and children in four ships to colonise the Solomon Islands (find  Marquesas instead)
Spain and the Netherlands sign a twelve year Resistant Pact
End of the Twelve Years' Truce between Spain and the Netherlands
First public art exhibition at the Palais-Royal in Paris
French explorer René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle claims lower Mississippi (Louisiana) for France
French troops occupy Mons in the Spanish Netherlands after a siege during the Nine Years' War
British mariner Robert Jenkins' ear is cut off by Spanish Guarde Costa in the Caribbean, catalyst for a later war between Britain and Spain
Tippu Sahib drives out British from Bednore, India
Great Britain ratifies the Treaty of Paris, signed September 3, 1783, ending the American Revolutionary War
Mayor Wolters offers French King Louis Napoleon townhall as a palace
African Methodist Episcopal Church organizes (Philadelphia)
Danzig (Gdańsk) dike break flood kills 1,200
First US tax-supported public library opens in Peterborough, New Hampshire
UK National Gallery re-opens in its new dedicated building in Trafalgar Square, London
Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville uses his phonautograph to make a 10-second recording of "Au Clair de la Lune"
Battle of Pleasant Hill, Louisiana, Confederate assault repulsed by Union side with high cost of estimated 3,100 causalities
Union surgeon Mary Edwards Walker is captured by Confederate troops and arrested as a spy during US Civil War
Hudson's Bay Company cedes its territory to Canada
American Anti-Slavery Society dissolves
Samuel R Percy patents dried milk
Greek shooter Pantelis Karasevdas scores a perfect 40 hits to win the military rifle event at the Athens Olympics; Greek 1-2-3 with Pavlos Pavlidis and Nicolaos Trikupis taking out the minor placings
The US Congress pass the Payne-Aldrich bill, raising certain tariffs on goods entering the United States
First recorded sighting in 1910 of Halley's Comet, seen in Gore, New Zealand
1st exhibition baseball game at Fenway Park (Red Sox vs Harvard)
Brooklyn Superbas' (later Dodgers) Ebbets Field opens; visiting Philadelphia Phillies win, 1-0
"World, the Flesh & the Devil", 1st color film, shown in London
Tampico incident - US ship crew arrested in Mexico
Battle of Arras begins
In 'Adkins vs Children's Hospital', the US Supreme Court finds that the minimum wage law for women and children, adopted by the District of Columbia, is unconstitutional
Sean O'Casey's "Shadow of a Gunman" premieres in Dublin
Top-Oss soccer team forms in Oss
Turkey passes separation of church & state
Stanley Cup Final, Maple Leaf Gardens, Toronto, ON: Toronto Maple Leafs beat New York Rangers, 6-4 for 3-0 series sweep; Toronto's first SC as the Maple Leafs
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Montreal Maroons beat Toronto Maple Leafs, 4-1 for 3-0 series sweep; Maroons last defunct team to win the Cup
German cruiser Blucher torpedoed and capsizes in Oslofjord, 1,000 die
Nazi Germany invades Denmark and Norway, and Denmark surrenders after a six-hour battle
LPGA Titleholders Championship Women's Golf, Augusta CC: Dorothy Kirby wins comfortably by 16 strokes ahead of Helen Sigel
PGA establishes Golf Hall of Fame
Battle of Bataan; US-Filipino forces overwhelmed by Japanese at Bataan
Battleship Admiral Scheer sunk by RAF bombing in Kiel
Liberty ship at Bari Italy carrying aerial bombs explodes, kills 360
NFL requires players to wear long stockings
World War II: The Battle of Königsberg, East Prussia, ends.
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Montreal Canadiens beat Boston Bruins, 6-3 for a 4-1 series victory
Atomic Energy Commission confirmed
Tornadoes striking West Texas and Oklahoma kill 169, and injure 1,300
Massacre at Deir Yassin.
UN International Court of Justice holds Albania responsible for incidents in Corfu Channel and awards Britain damages
4th Tony Awards: "The Cocktail Party" (play) & "South Pacific" (musical) win
Hugo Ballivian's government is overthrown by the Bolivian National Revolution, starting a period of agrarian reform, universal suffrage and the nationalisation of tin mines
7th Cannes Film Festival: "Gate of Hell" directed by Teinosuke Kinugasa wins the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
WECT TV channel 6 in Wilmington, NC (NBC/CBS) begins broadcasting
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
13th NBA Championship: Boston Celtics sweep Minneapolis Lakers in 4 games, first of Boston Celtics' record 8 straight titles
Baltimore Orioles pull their 2nd triple play (3-6-3 vs Washington Senators)
NASA names 1st 7 astronauts for Project Mercury
South African Prime Minister Hendrik Verwoerd survives an assassination attempt by David Pratt despite being twice shot in the face
Beatles "Ticket to Ride" single is released in UK
Anaheim Stadium for California Angels opens
1st Boeing 737 (a 100 series) makes its maiden flight
Shortwave broadcaster Radio NY Worldwide's transmitter burns down
German DR adopts constitution
Minn's Wayne Connelly is 1st to score on a Stanley Cup penalty shot
Ralph Aberbathy elected to head So Christian Leadership Conference
1st flight of Concorde 002 (Filton-Bristol)
MLB Chicago Cubs Billy Williams hits 4 consecutive doubles in 11-3 win over the Philadelphia , at Wrigley Field in Chicago
USSR & Iraq sign friendship treaty
Netherlands recognizes North Vietnam
Otto Kerner, former governor of Illinois, convicted for his role in an illegal racetrack scheme
24 OECD members sign an agreement to establish a $25 billion lending facility to provide assistance to industrial nations hurt by high oil prices
First appearance of sports mascot, the San Diego Chicken
US & Russia agreed on size of nuclear tests for peaceful use
Communist party in Spain allowed legally after 40 years
Brewers sweep Orioles 11-3, 16-3, & 13-5 (each with a grand slam)
Belgium's Marten's government resigns
Los Angeles Kings tie NHL record with two shorthanded playoff goals (vs NY Islanders)
Soyuz 35 carries 2 cosmonauts to Salyut 6
US Navy nuclear submarine USS George Washington rams Japanese freighter Nisso Maru, sinking the civilian ship in the South China Sea
LA Lakers block 21 Denver shots setting NBA regulation game record
6th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Challenger 1 returns to Earth
Canada Health Act, the country's public funded heath insurance scheme (Medicare), passed by the Canadian House of Commons
NHL New Jersey Devils win 3-0 over New York Islanders; Devils lead 2-1 in playoff 1st round
US imposes economic sanctions on Panama
Washington, D.C. march supporting 1973 Roe vs Wade decision (allow abortions)
"Capital News" starring Lloyd Bridges premieres on ABC-TV
NY Islanders beat NY Rangers 4-3 in double overtime-Rangers lead 2-1
World's largest bunny hop at Radio City Music Hall, New York City; record eclipsed in 2007
Georgia SSR votes to secede from USSR
Florida drops rape charges against NY Mets Gooden, Boston & Coleman
Record 18 golfers shoot in 60s in Masters round 1 (old record 12)
William O Studeman, becomes deputy director of CIA
Colorado Rockies 1st home game & 1st victory, 11-4 over Mont Expos
BPAA US Open by Justin Hromek
STS-59 (Endeavour) launches into orbit
Cleveland Indians pitcher Jose Mesa is acquitted of rape
Major League Soccer announces Miami & Chicago expansion
NFL announces it will give $3M to CFL & possible "World Classic Bowl"
Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara, President of Niger, is assassinated at the airport in Niamey, Niger
Gunman murders five people, injures eleven, and commits suicide in a mall in the Netherlands
"The Lion King" becomes highest grossing Broadway show, overtaking "The Phantom of the Opera"
12 civilians and UN peacekeepers are killed in an ambush in Jonglei, Sudan
13 people are killed and 3 are injured from a shooting spree in Velika Ivanča, Serbia
37 people are killed and 850 are injured after a magnitude 6.1 earthquake strikes Iran
The French Senate approves a bill for same-sex marriage
Stuart Parkin is awarded the Millennium Technology Prize for his work on magnetic storage
Suicide car bombing in Mogadishu, Somalia kills at least 17, Al-Shabaab group claim responsibility
Twitter footage of passenger forcibly removed from United Airlines flight in Chicago after flight overbooked causes outrage
Two Egyptian coptic churches in Tanta and Alexandria attacked by suicide bombers leaving at least 44 dead
Nine prominent Hong Kong pro-democracy protesters found guilty on public nuisance charges for their part in 2014 "Umbrella Movement"
Wolves have returned to the Netherlands after 140 years claim ecologists
La Soufrière volcano begins erupting on the Caribbean island of St. Vincent with a plume of ash 20,000 feet into the sky
San Diego Padres pitcher Joe Musgrove no-hits the Texas Rangers, 3-0 at Globe Life Field, Arlington, TX
Joel Embiidis named the NBA scoring champion, averaging 30.6 points per game
Joel Embiidis named the NBA scoring champion for the second straight season, averaging 33.1 points per game
Kuwaiti media outlet, Kuwaiti News unveils virtual news presenter on Twitter speaking Arabic, using artificial intelligence (although China was the 1st in 2018)
A group of older Swiss women win a landmark climate change case at the European Court of Human Rights, ruling the women's rights had been violated by not fully addressing climate change
Arizona Supreme Court upholds a 1864 law banning all abortions except to save a woman's life
James and Jennifer Crumbley, the first parents held criminally responsible for a US mass school shooting committed by their child, are sentenced to 10-15 years for manslaughter in Michigan
Simon Harris becomes Ireland's youngest taoiseach at 37 after the resignation of Leo Varadkar
Septimius Severusis proclaimed Roman Emperor by the army in Illyricum
Louis the Pious, King of the Franks, barely survives when wooden gallery collapses in Aachen, prompts him to later name his succession
Henry Vcrowned King of England at Westminster Abbey

Edward V, aged 12, succeeds his fatherEdward IVas King of England. He is never crowned and disappears, presumed murdered, after being incarcerated in the Tower of London with his younger brother Richard (the "Princes in the Tower")
Danish KingChristian IIIjoins the Schmalkaldic League
John Hancockrefuses to allow two British customs agents to go below deck of his ship, considered by some to be the first act of physical resistance to British authority in the colonies
Philosopher and mathematicianJean-Baptiste Le Rond d'Alembertbecomes permanent secretary of the French Academy of Sciences
Confederate GeneralRobert E. Leeand 26,765 troops surrender at Appomattox Court House to U.S. Lieutenant General Ulysses S. Grant, ending the Civil War in Northern Virginia
Civil Rights Bill passes over PresidentAndrew Johnson's veto
1st performance ofAnton Bruckner's 5th Symphony in B in Graz, Austria-Hungary, abridged and re-orchestrated by conductor Franz Schauk without the composer's approval
Australian athleteEdwin Flackclaims the middle distance double at the Athens Olympics winning the 800m final in 2:11.0; his 2nd victory of the Games after success in the 1500m
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonrefuses to recognizeVictoriano Huertaas President of Mexico on the ground that the dictator has not been elected by the people
Vimy Ridgein France stormed by Canadian troops
At the height of his fameBabe Ruthrushed to hospital with a mysterious condition labelled “the bellyache heard round the world". Ruth reported dead by some newspapers.
Eugene O'Neill's play "Lazarus Laughed" premieres in Pasadena, with a masked chorus of over 100
Mae West's NYC debut in a daring new play "Diamond Lil"
Stanley Cup Final Game Three: Chicago Black Hawks defeat Montreal Canadiens 3-2 (Canadians eventually win in best of five series)
German banking regulatorHans Lutheris shot and suffers a shoulder wound at the Potsdam, Germany train station by an assailant who disliked his fiscal policies

American contraltoMarian Andersonsings before 75,000 at the Lincoln Memorial in Washington, D.C; appearance organized after Anderson was denied permission to sing to an integrated audience in Constitution Hall
Alexander Gretchaninov's 4th Symphony premieres at Carnegie Hall, NYC, with John Barbirolli conducting the New York Philharmonic
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Orientals Ecclesiae
Baseball Commissioner Happy Chandler suspends Brooklyn DodgerLeo Durocherfor 1 year for "association with known gamblers"
Bob Hope's 1st TV appearance
Howard Hanson's "Song of Democracy" premieres in Washington, D.C.
Bill Sharmanhits a NBA record 56 consecutive foul shot
34th Academy Awards: "West Side Story" (Best Picture, Director, and 8 more),Sophia Loren(Two Women) &Maximilian Schell(Judgement At Nuremberg) win
US President JohnF. Kennedy throws out 1st ball at Washington's new DC Stadium
Winston Churchillbecomes 1st honorary US citizen
1st baseball game at Houston Astrodome, Astros beat NY Yankees 2-1 in exhibition asMickey Mantlehits MLB's 1st indoor homerun; US President Lyndon Johnson and wife Lady Bird attend
Martin Luther King Jr., buried in Atlanta
Ringo Starrreleases single "It Don't Come Easy", produced byGeorge Harrison, in the UK

"Paper Moon" film starring Ryan O'Neal and his daughter, Tatum O'Neal, directed byPeter Bogdanovichpremieres in Hollywood, California; Tatum O'Neal becomes youngest (age 10) Academy Award winner (Supporting Actress)
San Diego Padres ownerRay Krocaddresses fans, "Ladies and gentlemen, I suffer with you... I've never seen such stupid ball playing in my life!"

"All the President's Men", directed by Alan J. Pakula, based on the non-fiction book by journalistsCarl BernsteinandBob Woodwarddetailing their Watergate investigation, starringDustin HoffmanandRobert Redford, is released
In separate NBA games Denver's David Thompson scores 73 points & San Antonio'sGeorge Gervinscores 63 points (33 in 1 quarter)
51st Academy Awards: "The Deer Hunter",Jon Voight&Jane Fondawin
Los Angeles DodgersFernando Valenzuelamakes MLB debut as opening day starter due to pitching staff injuries, beats Astros 2-0; goes on to win both Rookie of the Year and Cy Young Awards
David Bowie's single "Let's Dance" becomes his 1st song to hit #1 in both the US and the UK
56th Academy Awards: "Terms of Endearment",Robert Duvall&Shirley Maclainewin
56th Academy Awards:Linda Huntbecomes the first person to win an Oscar for portraying a character of the opposite sex, Billy Kwan in The Year of Living Dangerously (1982)
White Sox pitcherTom Seaverstarts a record 15th opening day game
TV show "Dallas" announces it will revive killed Bobby Ewing character
For 3rd time,Wayne Gretzky, scores 7 points (1 goal, 6 assists) in a Stanley Cup game and passes Jean Béliveau as all time playoff scoring champ
Mike Tysonstrikes a parking attendant when asked to move his car
MLB New York Yankees outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals his 800th career base in 4-3 loss to Cleveland Indians
Don Mattinglysigns a $19.7 million 5-year contract extension with New York Yankees
John Majoris elected Prime Minister of the United Kingdom after his Conservative Party wins the most votes in British electoral history
US Fed court finds Panamanian dictatorManuel Noriegaguilty of 8 out of 10 drug and racketeering charges

Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,Elton John,James Taylor,Whitney Houston,Luciano Pavarotti, Aaron Neville,Tammy Wynette, Branford Marsalis, and Larry Adler
Funeral ofQueen Elizabeth, the Queen Motherat Westminster Abbey UK. More than a million people line the streets
Mockumentary "Parks and Recreation" starringAmy Poehler, Nick Offerman andAziz Ansaridebuts on NBC in the US
Joel Embiiddeclares for the 2014 NBA draft, forgoing his final three years of college eligibility
Fleetwood Macannounce new members Neil Finn and Mike Campbell after firing long-standing member Lindsey Buckingham
US SenatorTammy Duckworthof Illinois becomes the first senator to give birth while in office
Uganda opposition party National Unity Platform Party, headed byBobi Wine, claims 623 people have been abducted and tortured by the government of President Yoweri Museveni
Kawhi Leonardleads the Los Angeles Clippers to a win over the Phoenix Suns, securing the fifth seed in the Western Conference playoff bracket
US PresidentDonald Trumpannounces reversal on global tariffs, proposing a 90-day pause–excluding China
The Libau sets sail from Germany with a cargo of 20,000 rifles to assist Irish republicans; Captain Karl Spindler changes the name of the vessel to the Aud to avoid British detection
Baghdad falls to US forces, ending the invasion of Iraq, resulting in widespread looting
John, Cardinal of Lorraine, French churchman, born in Bar-le-Duc, Lorraine (d. 1550)
John Davenport, Puritan clergyman and cofounder of the New Haven Colony, born in Coventry, Warwickshire, England (d. 1670)
Johann Crüger [Jan Krygar], Sorbian-German cantor, hymn composer, editor (Praxis pietatis melica), and educator, born in Groß Breesen, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1662)
Giacomo Maria Predieri, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1695)
Johann Kaspar Kerll, German composer, born in Adorf, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1693)
Albertine Agnes, princess of Orange-Nassau, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1696)
Henri de Massue, Marquis de Ruvigny, 1st Viscount Galway, French soldier and diplomat, born in Paris (d. 1720)
Philippe Néricault Destouches, French dramatist and author, born in Tours, France (d. 1754)
James Craggs the Younger, English politician, born in Westminster, London (d. 1721)
Johann Matthias Gesner, German classical scholar, born in Roth, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1761)
Johann Georg Zechner, Austrian composer, born in Gleisdorf, Austria (d. 1778)
Georg Matthias Monn, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1750)
Emanuel Schikaneder, German playwright and composer, born in Straubing, Duchy of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1812)
Pieter Paulus, Dutch Patriot and President of the First National Assembly in The Hague, born in Axel, Netherlands (d. 1796)
Antonio Frantisek Becvarovsky, Czech composer, born in Mlada Boleslav, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1823)
Wojciech Boguslawski, Polish composer, born in Glinno, Poznań County, Poland (d. 1829)
Thomas Johann Seebeck, German physicist (Seebeck effect), born in Tallinn, Estonia, Russian Empire (d. 1831)
Étienne Aignan, French writer, born in Beaugency, France (d. 1824)
Sibrandus Stratingh, Dutch physician and chemist (electric car), born in Adorp, Netherlands (d. 1841)
George Peacock, English mathematician (algebraic theory), born in County Durham, England (d. 1858)
Aristide Farrenc, French flautist, music publisher, and musicologist (Trésor des pianistes), born in Marseilles, France (d. 1865)
Theobald Boehm, German inventor of the modern flute, born in Munich, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1881)
Giuditta Pasta, Italian soprano, born in Saronno, Cisalpine Republic (d. 1865)
Randolph Barnes Marcy, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Greenwich, Massachusetts (d. 1887)
Charles-Pierre Baudelaire, French poet and translator (Les Fleurs du mal), born in Paris, France (d. 1867)
Thomas Hewson Neill, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1885)
Eadweard Muybridge, English photographer, pioneer in study of motion, born in Kingston upon Thames, England (d. 1904)
Francis William Davenport, English composer, born in Wilderslowe, Derby, England (d. 1925)
Paolo Tosti, Italian composer, born in Ortona, Italy (d. 1916)
Francis MacKinnon, English cricket batsman (1 Test; Kent CCC) and 35th Chief of MacKinnon clan, born in London, England (d. 1947)
Helene Lange, German feminist (honoured with the Grand Prussian State Medal), born in Oldenburg, Germany (d. 1930)
Hermann Zumpe, German composer, born in Oppach, Germany (d. 1903)
C. E. A. Wichmann, German-Dutch geologist, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1927)
Seaborn McDaniel Denson, American composer, born in Cleburne County, Alabama (d. 1936)
Gyula Reviczky, Hungarian author and poet, born in Kuzmice, Topoľčany District, Slovakia (d. 1889)
Joseph Hellmesberger Jr., Austrian composer, violinist, and conductor, born in Vienna, Austrian Empire (d. 1907)
Charles Holroyd, English artist and curator, Director of the National Gallery (1906-16), born in Potternewton, Leeds (d. 1917)
Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti, English electrical engineer and inventor, born in Liverpool, England (d. 1930)
Charles Proteus Steinmetz, German-American electrical engineer (development of alternating current), born in Breslau, Province of Silesia, Prussia (d. 1923)
Chris Watson, third Prime Minister of Australia (Labour Party), born in Valparaíso, Chile (d. 1941)
Julius Bittner, Austrian composer (Little Violet; Missa Austriaca), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1939)
George Shepstone, South African cricket batsman (2 Tests; Transvaal), born in Pietermaritzberg, Natal, South Africa (d. 1940)
Gerald Kelly, British painter, born in London (d. 1972)
Frederick Francis IV, Grand Duke of Mecklenburg-Schwerin, born in Palermo, Italy (d. 1945)
Frank King, American cartoonist (Gasoline Alley), born in Cashton, Wisconsin (d. 1969)
Renzo Bossi, Italian composer, and educator (Milan Conservatory), born in Como, Italy (d. 1965)
Franco Vittadini, Italian composer, born in Pavia, Italy (d. 1948)
Sol Hurok, American theatrical impresario, born in Pogar, Chernigov Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1974)
John Gobau, Flemish actor (Electricity, Hostage Rights, The Cross-Patch), born in Antwerpen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1958)
Edmund Jenkins, American clarinetist and composer (Charlestonia; Afram), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1926)[1]
Ernest Kanitz, Austrian-American composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1978)
Beau De Glen "Mance" Lipscomb, American blues singer and guitarist ("Shine On, Harvest Moon"), born in Navasota, Texas (d. 1976)
Michel Simon, Swiss actor (Panic, Plague, Two of Us), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1975)
Rudolf Kattnigg, Austrian composer, born in Carinthia, Austria (d. 1955)
Julius Patzak, Austrian tenor singer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1974)
James Smith McDonnell, American aviator and engineer, born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1980)
Allen Jenkins, American actor (Hey Jeannie, Top Cop, Girl Habit), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 1974)
Paul Willis, American actor (The Little Lumberjack, Little Kaintuck), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1960)
Sharon Lynn [D'Auvergne Sharon Lindsay], American silent and sound screen actress (Way Out West; The Big Broadcast), born in Weatherford, Texas (d. 1963)
František Suchý, Czech oboist and composer (Moravian Wind Quintet), born in Libina, Bohemia, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1977)
Gregory Pincus, American biologist and inventor (birth control pill), born in Woodbine, New Jersey (d. 1903)
Stanislaw Wigura, Polish airplane designer and aviator (co-winner of international 'Challenge 1932'), born in Warsaw, Congress Poland (d. 1932)
Ward Bond, American actor (Wagon Train, It's a Wonderful Life), born in Benkelman, Nebraska (d. 1960)
Willem Pee, Belgian linguist, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 1986)
Joseph "Sharkey" Bonano, American Dixieland jazz trumpeter and singer, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1972)
Lyle Latell, American actor (Not of This Earth; Dick Tracy's Dilemma; Sky Dragon), born in Alma, Iowa (d. 1967)
J. William Fulbright, American senator from Arkansas, known for opposition to the Vietnam War), born in Sumner, Missouri (d. 1995)
Antal Doráti, Hungarian-American conductor (Minneapolis Symphony, 1949-1960; National Symphony, 1970-77), and composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1988)
Hugh Gaitskell, British politician (Leader of the Labour Party), born in Kensington, London (d. 1963)
Rafaela Aparicio [Rafaela Díaz Valiente], Spanish actress, born in Málaga, Spain (d. 1996)
Victor Vasarely, Hungarian-French painter and author (Op Art), born in Pécs, Hungary, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1997)
Fred Lohse, German composer, born in Leipzig, Germany (d. 1987)
Domenico Enrici, Roman Catholic Curia Archbishop, born in Cervasca, Italy (d. 1997)
Ivan Ivonovich Dzerzhinsky, Russian composer, born in Tambov, Dzerzhinsky, Russian Empire (d. 1978)
Robert Helpmann, Australian actor, choreographer and dancer (Chitty Chitty Bang Bang, Don Quixote), born in Mount Gambier, South Australia (d. 1986)
Abraham Ribicoff, American politician, U.S. Senator from Connecticut, born in New Britain, Connecticut (d. 1998)
Richard Arthur de Yarburgh-Bateson, 6th Lord Deramore, British architect, born in London (d. 2006)
Lew Kopelew, Russian author, born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1997)
Wilson Charles Geoffrey Baldwin, hero, born in Tendring, Essex (d. 1994)
Albert Rémy, French actor (Grand Prix, The 400 Blows, The Train), born in Sèvres, Seine, France (d. 1967)
Bob Quinn, Australian Football Hall of Fame rover (All Australian captain 1947; SA captain 1945-47; Magarey Medal 1938, 45; Port Adelaide FC), born in Adelaide, South Australia (d. 2008)
Daniel Johnson, Sr., Canadian politician, 20th Premier of Quebec, born in Danville, Quebec, Canada (d. 1968)
Julian Dash, American swing jazz tenor saxophonist, and composer ("Tuxedo Junction"), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1974)
Brad Dexter [Boris Milanovich], American actor (Macao, The Magnificent Seven), born in Goldfield, Nevada (d. 2002)
Johannes Bobrowski, German poet and writer, born in Tilsit, East Prussia (d. 1965)
Vincent O'Brien, Irish racehorse trainer (6 x Epsom Derby; 2 x British champion trainer; only British Triple Crown winner, Nijinsky 1970), born in Churchtown, Ireland (d. 2009)
Jørn Utzon, Danish architect (Sydney Opera House), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2008)
J. Presper Eckert, Jr., American engineer and co-inventor (1st electronic computer-ENIAC), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1995)
Alexander Moulton, English engineer and bicycle designer (folding bicycle), born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England (d. 2012)
Art Van Damme, American jazz accordionist (Chicago Jazz), born in Norway, Michigan (d. 2010)
F. Don Miller, American sports executive (executive director USOC 1973-85; President US Olympic Foundation 1985-96), born in Racine, Wisconsin (d. 1996)
William Raynor, American screenwriter (Gomer Pyle; Get Smart; McDuff, The Talking Dog; Dukes of Hazzard), born in Queens, New York (d. 1994)
Frankie Thomas, American actor and author (Tom Corbett Space Cadet), born in New York City (d. 2006)
Jean-Marie Balestre, French president of motor sport's FIA, born in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence, France (d. 2008)
Yitzhak Navon, President of Israel (1978-83), born in Jerusalem, British Mandate of Palestine (d. 2015)
Carl Amery [Christian Anton Mayer], German writer, born in Munich (d. 2005)
Gerald Moverley, first Bishop of the Diocese of Hallam in Yorkshire, born in Bradford, England (d. 1996)
Michael Palliser, head of British diplomatic service, born in Reigate, Surrey (d. 2012)
Bruno Kiefer, German-born Brazilian composer, born in Baden-Baden, Germany (d. 1987)
Dann Cahn, American film editor (I Love Lucy, Beverly Hillbillies), born in Hollywood, California (d. 2012)
Harald Heilmann, German composer, born in Aue, Germany (d. 2018)
Art Kane [Kanofsky], American art director (Seventeen Magazine), and portrait photographer (A Great Day In Harlem), born in New York City (d. 1995)[1]
Michael Richardson, British investment banker and advisor to Margaret Thatcher known as "Mr Privatisation", born in London (d. 2003)
Tom Jackson, British union leader (Post Office), born in Leeds, West Yorkshire (d. 2003)
Graham Hills, English physical scientist and principal (Strathclyde University, England), born in Southend-on-Sea, Essex (d. 2014)
Harris Wofford, American politician (Sen-D-Pennsylvania 1991-94), head of AmeriCorps, born in New York City (d. 2019)
Michael Ogden, British barrister (Ogden Tables), born in London (d. 2003)
Brian Cubbon, British senior civil servant, born in Lancashire, England (d. 2015)
Floyd Spence, American attorney and politician (Rep-R-SC, 1971-2001), born in Columbia, South Carolina (d. 2001)
Monty Sunshine, British jazz clarinetist and bandleader (Petite Fleur), born in Stepney, London (d. 2010)
Tom Lehrer, American musician ("The Elements"), satirist (That Was The Week That Was), and mathematician, born in New York City (d. 2025)[1][2]
Harvey Lichtenstein, American head of the Brooklyn Academy of Music, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2017)
F. Albert Cotton, American chemist, born in Philadelphia Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
John Bartholomew Tucker, American TV host (Candid Camera, 1974-75; Treasure Island, 1967-68), and author, born in Pennsylvania (d. 2014)
Nathaniel Branden, Canadian-American psychotherapist, born in Brampton, Ontario, Canada (d. 2014)
Bill Gilbert, American sports and nature writer (Sports Illustrated, National Geographic), born in Kalamazoo, Michigan (d. 2012)
Richard Hatfield, Canadian politician (26th Premier of New Brunswick), born in Hartland, New Brunswick (d. 1991)

Abdul Mati [Matias Klarwein], French artist known for his works used on music album covers (Santana's Abraxus; Miles Davis' Bitches Brew; Osibisa's Heads; Jon Hassel's Dream Theory), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 2002)
